## Background

This notebook derives and implements the physics model for the fragmentation field
produced by a detonating HE artillery shell — Phase 1 MVP of the WW2 field artillery
demonstration. Three sub-models are integrated:

1. **Gurney equation** — initial fragment velocity from explosive energy and M/C mass ratio.
1. **Mott distribution** — number and mass distribution of fragments from shell break-up statistics.
1. **Drag decay** — fragment kinetic energy vs. ground distance, assuming flat (horizontal) trajectories.

**Scope constraints and known limitations:**

- Single HE shell; fuse type deferred; default is a ground burst with no airburst height.
- Fragment trajectories assumed flat (horizontal); gravity is not in the equations of
  motion. Checked and found negligible inside the model's ~200 m operating envelope —
  see the [gravity-omission assessment](challenges/gravity.html) for the governing
  equations and numbers; beyond ~250 m gravity would dominate, but the field is already
  sub-lethal there.
- Uniform azimuthal spray: fragments are spread uniformly in 360° around the burst point.
  This is accurate only if the shell detonates with its **axis vertical** (90° angle of fall,
  i.e. pure plunging fire). At shallower angles of fall — typical of field artillery at
  medium ranges (angle of fall ≈ 10°–50°) — the pattern becomes an ellipse elongated along
  the line of fire, with more fragments projected forward and aft than to the sides. The
  angle-of-fall correction is deferred to a later model iteration.
- Shell dimensions and mass are fully used: caliber and wall thickness drive the break-up
  radius → Mott half-mass; shell mass and filler mass drive the Gurney M/C ratio → initial
  velocity.
- No obstacles; flat absorbing surface.

The default parameters are for a **WW2 105 mm M1 HE shell** loaded with TNT.

______________________________________________________________________
